fr:mainmatter.html:p: This week I spent the large majority of my time taking notes in this forest. We can see this via the 23hr spent on my forest throughout the week. Admittedly, some of this was spent purely on aesthetic re-design / cleanup, which I hope to not have to do this week. Lab corresponds to my kernel learning, where I spent what appears to be ~5hr implementing a few of the Triton tutorials, including GEMM. I played around with excalidraw and took some notes here. With this, I began looking into the Kernel DSLs landscape along with MLIR.

Outside of coding, I did not spend too much time brain-rotting. My phone screentime has also gotten remarkably low, so the issue is not really wasting time doomscrolling / watching youtube, etc. Instead, the problem is just a lack of maintained focus. I bounce between things too frequently, failing to get as much done as I should. I hope to remedy this by being disciplined with pomodoro blocks but early attempts have not been too promising.

fr:title.text: Triton: An intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Triton: An intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @inproceedings{tilletTritonIntermediateLanguage2019, title = {Triton: An Intermediate Language and Compiler for Tiled Neural Network Computations}, author = {Tillet, Philippe and Kung, H. T. and Cox, David}, year = {2019}, isbn = {978-1-4503-6719-6}, doi = {10.1145/3315508.3329973}, urldate = {2026-06-23}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the 3rd {{ACM SIGPLAN International Workshop}} on {{Machine Learning}} and {{Programming Languages}}}, pages = {10--19}, publisher = {ACM}, address = {Phoenix AZ USA}, file = {/home/kellen/Downloads/papers/Triton an intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations - Tillet et al..pdf}, langid = {english}, abstract = {The validation and deployment of novel research ideas in the field of Deep Learning is often limited by the availability of efficient compute kernels for certain basic primitives. In particular, operations that cannot leverage existing vendor libraries (e.g., cuBLAS, cuDNN) are at risk of facing poor device utilization unless custom implementations are written by experts -- usually at the expense of portability. For this reason, the development of new programming abstractions for specifying custom Deep Learning workloads at a minimal performance cost has become crucial.}, month = {June}, shorttitle = {Triton} }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tile-based Kernel DSLs lowered to backend agnostic MLIR.
This is the first official entry into my Weeknotes! The infrastructure has long been in development, but I have been too lazy / scared to actually start making / publicizing these.

This week I spent the large majority of my time taking notes in this forest. We can see this via the 23hr spent on my forest throughout the week. Admittedly, some of this was spent purely on aesthetic re-design / cleanup, which I hope to not have to do this week. Lab corresponds to my kernel learning, where I spent what appears to be ~5hr implementing a few of the Triton tutorials, including GEMM. I played around with excalidraw and took some notes here. With this, I began looking into the Kernel DSLs landscape along with MLIR.

Outside of coding, I did not spend too much time brain-rotting. My phone screentime has also gotten remarkably low, so the issue is not really wasting time doomscrolling / watching youtube, etc. Instead, the problem is just a lack of maintained focus. I bounce between things too frequently, failing to get as much done as I should. I hope to remedy this by being disciplined with pomodoro blocks but early attempts have not been too promising.

I have been on a remarkable streak of bad luck when trying to run. During vacation (the 3 mile week), there was a heatwave going on throughout Europe. Then, upon my return, I got wildly sick (still managed to get to 30 miles but my Garmin did not like it). Then, this most recent week the Canadian wildfires made Michigan look like a scene out of Chernobyl. I tried to run, but my eyes began watering as soon as I got outside. Hopefully, I can get back on track and scale back up to 40mpw and then to 50+ before we get too close to the marathon.

In other news, I seem to be getting pretty consistent with my habits. I have remarkably caught up on my Anki deck and now need to do more exploratory study to have enough cards each day. The to-do list has not been as life-changing as I hoped as I still struggle to adhere to it on a daily basis. One reason is I think I am too ambitious and should instead start with just one must get done thing each day.

I have begun reading Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Goden Braid and am really enjoying it. It is strangely refreshing having the math I learned so rigorously distilled into much more digestible chunks. I have also gotten halfway through Candide, which is a very different read (thoughts coming soon).

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I completed a rough outline of the state of kernel generations as far as I am concerned. I hope to make some serious progress on this next week. Namely, I want to have a pretty complete understanding of the Tiled DSLs portion and where the distinction between each DSL is made. I would like to deeply understand the Flash attention Triton kernel from the tutorial. Afterwards, moving to TileLang and seeing how / where they draw the line for more optimization opportunity.
For Reading Corner, I will likely finish Candide and might also finish Elon Musk, which I have been working on for awhile now. Stay tuned for their reviews!
Dialects. This is effectively a syntax. For your given DSL, you must write each of the operations in this MLIR dialect in order to inherit the benefits of the MLIR ecosystem.
fr:mainmatter.html:blockquote:
The whole structured CodeGen paradigm in MLIR prefers co-designing op semantics and transformations and let ops structurally encode and guarantee certain properties to simplify analyses and transformations.
— Lei Zhang
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The general paradigm. It seems that when you are writing a DSL backed by MLIR that you

html:ol.html:li: Write an MLIR dialect for your DSL
You can write custom compiler passes for your MLIR dialect.

html:ul.html:li: This is what is done in Triton.
Triton has two custom MLIR dialects: ttir and ttgir.
The ttir dialect is the backend agnostic IR.
The ttgir dialect is the Triton GPU IR.

html:ol.html:li: Write a lowering pass from your MLIR dialect to the MLIR dialect of an MLIR with codegen backing i.e.

html:ul.html:li: Alternatively, XLA ingests StableHLO which is an MLIR dialect. In theory, this means that if your DSL is similar to that of a JAX / LinAlg dialect, then you can get a lot of the optimizations Google has come up with for free.

However, XLA works by translating StableHLO to HLO, which is then optimized by XLA. HLO is NOT an MLIR dialect and therefore in order to use XLA your backend needs to have an appropriate conversion from HLO to an executable on their backend.
This is what is done for trainium. Namely, the neuronx-cc compiler performs the translation from optimized HLO to optimized executable code on their ASIC.
Triton and the GPU backend for XLA are the LLVM + NVVM MLIR, which is the MLIR dialect for LLVM with added GPU instructions.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backlink accumulator for all things kernel DSLs.
An example of how to tile a matrix multiplication, our tile partitions the output matrix M \times N by block sizes BS_N and BS_M as seen in the image below (drawn using excalidraw)
The block size of k BS_K is then basically the accumulation axis, where we loop over k if we cannot fit the whole row / column of A and B into shared memory.
